retrieved_docs.title: Docetaxel -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In combination with cisplatin, docetaxel has been approved by the Federal Drug Agency (FDA) as a first-line agent in the treatment of prostate cancer. [1] Docetaxel is the standard of care in patients with castration-resistant prostate cancer for palliation and prolongation of life. [2] Single-dose docetaxel is generally first-line therapy in patients with non–small cell lung cancers (NSCLC) and poor prognosis. Due to a good tolerability profile, docetaxel is especially effective in preventing progression and extending survival in patients with NSCLC and metastatic disease. Docetaxel is a standard adjunct agent in breast cancer treatment, demonstrating significant improvements in survival in high-risk patients regardless of prognostic factors such as estrogen receptor expression, the degree of nodal involvement, age, menopause status, and schedule of administration. [3] Finally, in conjunction with oxaliplatin and capecitabine, docetaxel completes the triple-agent combination therapy known as the TEX regimen and is indicated for treating advanced gastric cancer. [4]  According to the American Society of Clinical Oncology guidelines for stage IV non–small cell lung cancer, for patients who have previously received both checkpoint inhibitor therapy and platinum-based doublet chemotherapy, single-agent docetaxel, with or without ramucirumab can be considered. [5] Combining anlotinib with docetaxel presents a viable therapeutic option for patients with advanced NSCLC who have not responded to initial platinum-based treatments. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Docetaxel -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Breast cancer: 75 mg/m 2 IV for a single dose on day 1 of a 21-day cycle; give 6 cycles as part of a multi-drug chemotherapy regimen. For locally advanced or metastatic disease monotherapy: 60 to 100 mg/m 2 on the first day of a 21-day cycle. For metastatic disease in combination therapy with capecitabine: 75 mg/m 2 IV for a single dose on day 1 of a 21-day cycle (for anthracycline-resistant disease)

retrieved_docs.title: Docetaxel -- Indications -- Breast cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic disease: Docetaxel exhibits single-agent activity in locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer, which has advanced after prior chemotherapy regimens. Adjuvant therapy: When combined with doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide, docetaxel improves outcomes in patients with operable, node-positive breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Doxorubicin -- Administration -- Adult dosing by indication:
retrieved_docs.content: Axillary node-positive breast cancer as an adjuvant: 60 mg/m^2 IV once on the first day of a 21-day cycle. Use with cyclophosphamide. Patients should receive 4 cycles. Leukemia monotherapy: 60 to 75 mg/m^2 IV once on the first day of a 21-day cycle. The maximum cumulative dose is 550 mg/m^2. (ALL or AML) Leukemia combination therapy: 40 to 75 mg/m^2 IV once on the first day of a 21-day or 29-day cycle. The maximum cumulative dose is 550 mg/m^2. (ALL or AML) Lymphoma monotherapy: 60 to 75 mg/m^2 IV once on the first day of a 21-day cycle. The maximum cumulative dose is 550 mg/m^2. (Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma) Lymphoma combination therapy: 40 to 75 mg/m^2 IV once on the first day of a 21-day or 29-day cycle. The maximum cumulative dose is 550 mg/m^2. (Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma) Solid tumors, monotherapy: 60 to 75 mg/m^2 IV once on the first day of a 21-day cycle. The maximum cumulative dose is 550 mg/m^2. Solid tumors, combination therapy:

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Atezolizumab is a humanized IgG1 monoclonal antibody that targets programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1). Atezolizumab has received FDA approval for multiple neoplastic conditions and may be administered as monotherapy or in combination with chemotherapy. Indications include locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma, metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, extensive-stage small cell lung cancer, metastatic triple-negative breast cancer, and unresectable or metastatic hepatocellular carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracyclines -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the indications for anthracycline therapy in a chemotherapeutic regimen. Identify the therapeutic mechanism of action of drugs in the anthracycline class. Outline the potential adverse events profile associated with anthracycline use. Review interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance appropriate clinical outcomes with anthracycline chemotherapy for cancer treatment and drive optimal outcomes with the least adverse effects. Explain the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients when using doxorubicin. retrieved_docs.title: Breast Cancer -- Medical Oncology -- Cytotoxic Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuvant chemotherapy is associated with improved overall survival, disease-free survival, and reduced local recurrence. [65] Cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, and 5-fluorouracil (CMF) combination was one of the early regimens used in the adjuvant treatment of breast cancer. More modern regimens use anthracyclines (eg, doxorubicin or epirubicin) and taxanes in regimens such as TAC (ie, docetaxel, adriamycin, and cyclophosphamide). Adjuvant chemotherapy is recommended for most patients with triple-negative and HER2-positive tumors that are >T1 stage. Treatment recommendations for HR-positive tumors are more nuanced and are guided by commercially available genetic analysis kits (eg, Oncotype Dx, Mammaprint). [66] [67] Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is increasingly used for triple-negative and HER2-positive tumors, which leads to increased compliance and tumor downstaging and allows assessment of the tumor's biological response. [68] [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Blastic Plasmacytoid Dendritic Cell Neoplasm -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The contraceptive and lactation needs of female patients should be explicitly reviewed. Patients should be instructed to weigh themselves daily on the same scale. Women of childbearing age receiving tagraxofusp should use an effective contraception method during therapy and for at least a week after completing their last treatment. Female patients who become pregnant should contact their doctor to seek alternate treatment choices. Waiting at least 48 hours following the previous dose before restarting breastfeeding is recommended. Although no studies on pregnant women have been conducted, there is a possible risk to the fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Taxane Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of taxane toxicity. Evaluate the common adverse effects of different taxanes. Differentiate the treatment of various adverse effects of taxane therapy. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients with taxane toxicity. retrieved_docs.content: Mitotane remains the only medication approved by the European Medicine Executive Agency (EMEA) and the U.S Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of “metastatic ACC.” An overview of collecting different studies showed that the objective response rate is at best, 24%. [102] [103] [104]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperthermia for Chest Wall Recurrence -- Radiation Oncology -- Hyperthermia, Chemotherapy, and Radiation (Triple Therapy)
retrieved_docs.content: Vujoskovic et al. evaluated the efficacy of neoadjuvant liposomal doxorubicin (30 to 75 mg/m), paclitaxel (100 to 175 mg/m), and hyperthermia, followed by resection and radiation therapy and then 8 cycles of CMF (cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, 5-fluorouracil) chemotherapy, in 47 patients with stage IIB-III locally advanced breast cancer. The authors found a combined (partial plus complete) clinical response of 72% and pathological response of 60% (4 patients achieved a pathologically complete response) 4-year DFS and OS were 63% and 75%, respectively [25] .

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoma -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: There are other systemic agents considered second-line agents after patients have failed anthracycline-based regimens. Temozolomide, an alkylating agent, has been utilized as a single agent in patients with advanced and pretreated STS. [49] [50] Another alkylating agent, trabectedin, is also indicated in patients who progressed on anthracycline-based therapy and were superior to the best supportive care. [51] Eribulin, a microtubule inhibitor, is another recommended second-line therapy for patients with advanced or metastatic STS and is a category-one recommendation for liposarcoma. [35] It has been evaluated for single agent versus dacarbazine use in multiple STS histologies, demonstrating a median overall survival benefit. [52]

retrieved_docs.content: Hormonal therapy : Anti-estrogen or aromatase inhibitor therapy is indicated in all hormone receptor-positive patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances is a pharmacologic class that includes the anthracycline agents aklarubicin, epirubicin, and idarubicin. These three compounds share a common tetracyclic quinone structure and are employed as cytotoxic chemotherapy for a variety of malignancies, with similar mechanisms of action and overlapping safety considerations such as potential cardiotoxicity. The ET protocol is specifically mapped from epirubicin, indicating that epirubicin serves as the foundational drug or key component of that treatment regimen. Recognizing that aklarubicin, epirubicin, and idarubicin belong to the anthracycline class aids clinicians in understanding their therapeutic roles and risks, while the connection of epirubicin to the ET protocol informs the selection and design of appropriate chemotherapy schedules in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1402
retrieved_docs.content: The R‑CHOP chemotherapy regimen incorporates cyclophosphamide as a direct component of its drug combination, contributing to the regimen’s overall antineoplastic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1304
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that share a common anthracycline core and are used primarily in chemotherapy. Within this class, the drugs aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron are recognized as individual members. All three agents intercalate into DNA, inhibit topoisomerase II, and generate free radicals, mechanisms that contribute to their cytotoxic effects against rapidly dividing cancer cells. Because they belong to the same pharmacologic group, aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron exhibit similar therapeutic indications, such as treatment of leukemias and solid tumors, as well as comparable adverse effect profiles, most notably dose‑dependent cardiotoxicity and myelosuppression. Understanding that aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron are members of the Anthracyclines and related substances class helps clinicians anticipate their pharmacodynamics, manage shared toxicities, and make informed decisions when selecting or substituting agents within this chemotherapy category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of chemotherapeutic agents that includes aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin, idarubicin and mitoxantron. These drugs share a common mechanism of action, typically involving DNA intercalation and inhibition of topoisomerase II, which underlies their effectiveness against a variety of malignant tumors. The ET protocol is directly linked to epirubicin, being mapped from this agent, which indicates that epirubicin serves as a foundational component of the ET treatment regimen. Understanding that aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin, idarubicin and mitoxantron are members of the anthracycline family helps clinicians select appropriate regimens and anticipate class‑related toxicities such as cardiotoxicity, while the association of the ET protocol with epirubicin guides its application in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that includes the individual drugs aklarubicin, epirubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron. These compounds share structural and pharmacologic characteristics typical of anthracycline chemotherapy, such as intercalation into DNA and inhibition of topoisomerase II, which contribute to their cytotoxic effects against rapidly dividing cancer cells. The ET protocol, a therapeutic regimen used in oncology, is derived from or mapped to epirubicin, indicating that epirubicin serves as the foundational agent for this specific treatment protocol. Understanding that aklarubicin, epirubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron belong to the anthracycline family helps clinicians select appropriate regimens and anticipate class‑related toxicities, while the linkage of the ET protocol to epirubicin guides its application in patient care.
